data.title: Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman, LLC Announces an Investigation Against Sun Communities, Inc. (SUI) and Encourages Stockholders to Learn More About the Investigation
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / April 17, 2025 / Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman, LLC is investigating potential claims on behalf of purchasers of Sun Communities, Inc. ("Sun Communities" or "the Company") . Investors who purchased Sun Communities securities prior to February 28, 2019, and continue to hold to the present, are encouraged to obtain additional information and assist the investigation by visiting the firm's site: bgandg.com/SUI.

Investigation Details

The investigation concerns whether Sun Communities and certain of its officers and/or directors have engaged in corporate wrongdoing.

What's Next?

If you are aware of any facts relating to this investigation or purchased Sun Communities shares, you can assist this investigation by visiting the firm's site: bgandg.com/SUI. You can also contact Peretz Bronstein or his client relations manager, Nathan Miller, of Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman, LLC:

There is No Cost to You

We represent investors in class actions on a contingency fee basis. That means we will ask the court to reimburse us for out-of-pocket expenses and attorneys' fees, usually a percentage of the total recovery, only if we are successful.

Why Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman

Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman, LLC is a nationally recognized firm that represents investors in securities fraud class actions and shareholder derivative suits. Our firm has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors nationwide.

Follow us for updates on LinkedIn, X, Facebook, or Instagram.

Attorney advertising. Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes.

data.description:

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

notice from copies of the content.

FBN INTERVIEW WITH JOHN WILLIAMS, PRESIDENT, NEW YORK FED

APRIL 17, 2025

SPEAKERS:

JOHN WILLIAMS, PRESIDENT, NEW YORK FED

EDWARD LAWRENCE, FBN CORRESPONDENT

EDWARD LAWRENCE, FOX BUSINESS CORRESPONDENT: Yes, Cheryl, and arguably the second most important person in the Federal Reserve System, John Williams, thank you for joining us on this. You know, the first question I want to ask you is you look at the market volatility over the past 14 days, but specifically yesterday and today, the futures are down again. Does that give you any pause then for possibly a rate cut again in May?

JOHN WILLIAMS, PRESIDENT, NEW YORK FED: Well, first of all, welcome back to the New York Fed. It's great to have you here. And you know, right now I look at where the economy is, the unemployment rate is 4.2 percent based on the latest data we have, it looks like inflation is running about 2.3 percent and, you know, the economy has — had grew very nicely last year, so the economy is in a very good place.

There's a lot of uncertainty out there about trade policy and other things. We are getting some information about that. But really, from my point of view, I think monetary policy is well positioned. I don't see any need to change the setting of the Fed funds rate anytime soon. And it's really about collecting information, understanding better what's happening in the economy during the rest of this year, understanding kind of how the uncertainty plays out and what it means for achievement of our maximum employment goals. But right now, I think monetary policy is in the right place.

LAWRENCE: But are you worried the Federal Reserve might fall behind in rate cutting cycle?

WILLIAMS: Well, I think that we have to look at all the data. You know, my view is based on what we know about tariffs that have been announced and kind of were expected and their effects on the economy, I think we have a period of higher inflation this year and a slower growth path for this year relative to last year. So that's a combination that you have to think carefully about.

What's the appropriate stance of policy? I obviously don't want to be late or in terms of any decision we make, but right now, I think we got policy in a good place and — and just got to collect that data and understand what's happening.

LAWRENCE: So with the rate cuts, the dot plot, the famed dot plot projected two rate cuts. The markets are now saying or thinking there might be four of 25 basis points. Where are you? Is — has your outlook changed in terms of how many rate cuts should be this year?

WILLIAMS: Well, I think it's really hard to say, because first of all, there's just a lot of uncertainty about how the economy is going to play out this year. I think also, you know, it really depends on how the economy responds, in terms of whatever policies happen, and also how inflation is behaving, and, importantly, how inflation expectations are behaving.

I will — I will put a little bit finer point on what the market expects. It. You look at the Fed Funds Futures, yes, something like three to four rate cuts this year. But if you look at kind of where the modal outlook is from, you know, we use options, prices to do that. You know, market expects one to two rate cuts this year. That hasn't actually changed over the last few months. So I think again, what we're seeing in market pricing is a reflection of the uncertainty about the economic outlook and what's going to happen in inflation.

So my own view is, I think that, you know, again, we're going to have to collect, watch the data, what it means for the outlook and the risk to that outlook. But you know, my basic views on where monetary policy is and should be haven't fundamentally changed.

LAWRENCE: You're talking about inflation, CPI inflation, month over month, actually decreased the first time in three years, a 0.1 percent does. Does that, you know, does that put your mind at ease a little bit, that maybe inflation is coming back down?

WILLIAMS: Well, it is. It's good news from the point of view of the — of the, you know, kind of gradual decline in inflation we've seen over the last year or two. It's a sign that maybe the little bit higher readings we saw in the first few months were outliers, and we're still on a disinflationary trend. So I view that as good news.

Still, we're above 2 percent inflation. We still have a ways to go on that. The bigger picture, of course, that's the rear view mirror. The bigger picture right now is how the various trade and other policies affect the inflation outlook.

LAWRENCE: The Fed Chairman said that he could see a one-time price increase with tariffs coming in. Do you see the one time price increase and then could it come back down?

WILLIAMS: Well, I think that you we will definitely get effects on prices and inflation this year from tariffs. That's what we've seen in the past. I think that's a pretty conventional view on how this will play out. I think the big unanswered question is, to what extent does the inflation remain high, say, in 2026 or even longer? We need to make sure that any one time changes in prices don't pass through into more persistent higher inflation. We need to get inflation back to 2 percent after this period, and do that on a sustained basis.

LAWRENCE: That sounds like a higher for longer in terms of rates?

WILLIAMS: Well, again, what the rate path is will depend on how the economy is behaving, what's happening in terms of our maximum employment, price stability goals. But essentially — it's — it's absolutely essential that we keep inflation expectations anchored through this. That's a lesson from history is something that's really paid dividends in the past, and that's really, quite honestly, where I'm focused.

LAWRENCE: Goldman Sachs raised the chance of a recession to 35 percent. Will we see a recession in the next 12 months?

WILLIAMS: Well, I'm not going to make a prediction around recessions or anything like that, because our objective is max — objective is for maximum employment, price stability. So of course, you know private sector economists, you know, are asked those questions a lot. I'm more focused on, what's the outlook for the economy? What are the some of the risks to that, and how do we get monetary policy positions?

So I definitely have a view that growth this year is going to be significantly slower than last year, probably somewhat below 1 percent GDP growth for the year, and unemployment will come up to about 4.5 to 5 percent so it is a slower economic outlook. That's not a recession. That's just a slower outlook, slower growth than we've seen in the past couple of years.

LAWRENCE: What do you — what are your CEO contacts telling you in terms of uncertainty? Are they still nervous about going forward?

WILLIAMS: No, absolutely. You know, I talk to business leaders, you know, from small businesses, medium businesses and global businesses. Uncertainty, and especially uncertainty around tariffs and trade policy is the number one topic. It's very difficult, if you're a business, to make long term decisions on investment, new products and things like that, when you're not quite sure what the environment will look like.

And for the U.S. economy, which is obviously very connected to the global economy and the global financial system, what I'm hearing is it's people kind of pausing on big business decisions and investment, waiting to see how this plays out, and trying to figure out how to be successful. You know, once we have that clarity.

LAWRENCE: In the last 30 seconds, we have the neutral rate. Has it fallen according to the Fed? Has our star come down?

WILLIAMS: Oh — according to the Fed, I can't speak to that. But right now, my estimates, you know, I do say the neutral rate is still relatively low. If you ask me about some of the changes we've seen in terms of the longer run, growth prospects for the economy and through immigration changes and other changes, I would think, if anything goes over, kind of keeping downward pressure on the neutral rate, my view is it hasn't. I don't have any evidence it's changed.

LAWRENCE: And where do you see this — the federal funds rate landing? Where is the bottom for you?

WILLIAMS: Well, my own view in terms of the — in the long run.

LAWRENCE: Yes.

WILLIAMS: You know, my view is with the 2 percent inflation, which we'll get back to. And then, you know, view of the longer run, the kind of neutral real rate is probably somewhere around three quarters of a percent or some plus or minus that. So we're talking about a Fed funds rate around 3 percent or a little bit less. That's just my view, but it could change.

LAWRENCE: John Williams, thank you very much for your time. I appreciate it.

END

data.title: Markets Defy Dollar's Safe Haven Status as Risk Assets PlungeAnalysis
data.description:
By Paulo Trevisani

A persistent weakening of the dollar is stoking fears that President Trump's erratic trade policy may have damaged the currency's safe-haven status, with consequences for the U.S. economy.

Amid the tariffs whiplash, investors have taken the typical approach of getting rid of risk assets, sending stock markets in a tailspin. Usually, they would do that while rushing to buy dollars, long viewed as a refuge for turbulent times.

Not this time. The S&P 500 fell 7% between April 2, a.k.a. Liberation Day, when Trump made the broadest tariffs proclamation thus far, and Wednesday. Meanwhile, the WSJ Dollar Index, which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies, fell 4% in the same period.

"A traditional correlation is changing pretty dramatically and very quickly," said Anders Persson, head of fixed income and Chief Investment Officer at Nuveen. He said the move was "really quite powerful."

The unusual move could be explained by a variety of factors, such as leveraged investors ditching dollar-denominated assets to raise cash in order to cover losses elsewhere. But some analysts believe that something deeper is happening: Investors no longer see the dollar as their best shelter.

"It could reflect some loss of confidence of foreigners in the U.S. economy," said Idanna Appio, portfolio manager at First Eagle Investments.

Moves in the dollar are closely related to Treasury yields, which are also seen as a safe haven. Foreign investors seeking refuge on Treasurys sell their local currency to get dollars needed to buy the securities. Their actions boost both the dollar value and bond prices, sending yields lower. After Liberation Day, however, yields moved higher as the dollar weakened. The 10-year benchmark rose to 4.5% on April 11 from 4.2% on April 2, and has since receded to 4.3%.

Another part of the puzzle is gold, which is rising as an alternative to U.S. assets. It rose 6% from April 2 through Wednesday, according to FactSet. The yen, also seen as a safe haven, has strengthened, with the dollar losing 5% of its value against the Japanese currency.

"Betting on continued USD strength against the yen has become increasingly risky," Rania Gule, Senior Market Analyst at XS.com, wrote in a press release Wednesday.

None of these alternatives are as easy to trade as the greenback and Treasurys, making it unlikely that they will take the place of U.S. assets so fast.

The search for safe havens other than the dollar and U.S. Treasurys has been going on even before last year's election. The most recent Treasury data available show that the top foreign holders of Treasurys, Japan and China, have reduced their holdings in the 12 months through February, although total foreign holdings have increased in the period.

But Trump's drastic reshuffling of the U.S. role in global trade could be accelerating the trend. Eventually, it could make it harder for Washington to spend its way out of economic downturns as it did in the pandemic.

The federal government has spent $1.31 trillion more than it took in so far this fiscal year, which ends Sept. 30, according to Treasury data. The gap was 23% wider than in the same period a year earlier.

The government issues Treasurys to cover the gap. If investors around the world get less interested in buying them, yields would go higher, pushing borrowing costs up across the economy.

"It would make our life more challenging because we are running very large fiscal deficits," Appio said.

In other words, the fallout from Trump's trade policy "reduces the U.S. government's ability to continue to provide its liabilities as the world's core asset without pushing up yields," Freya Beamish, chief economist at TS Lombard, wrote Wednesday.

"If the United States were to become a jurisdiction where risks are structurally higher going forwards, that would make us less attractive," Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said Wednesday when asked about the implications of lasting policy uncertainty. "We don't know that at this point, but I think that would be the effect."

Some observers are less gloomy. Economist Nouriel Roubini wrote on X that "over time equity capital will continue to flock from Europe and the whole rest of the world to the U.S. to finance the biggest capex boom in recent U.S. history as AI and other technologies/industries of the future will boom in the U.S."

Write to Paulo Trevisani at paulo.trevisani@wsj.com

Actum Pharma Expands Executive Team to Launch Enhanced Services Program to Support Biopharma Product Commercialization and Market Success
data.description:
BERWYN, Pa., April 17, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Actum Pharma (Actum), a strategy and operating business partner to global biopharma manufacturers, today announces two executive appointments to launch a new service platform designed to help life science companies fund, develop, manage and market novel, life-changing therapies. The addition of Donovan Quill, chief strategy officer of Life Sciences, and Bob Gilkin, RPh, MBA, chief commercial and growth officer, uniquely position Actum to address the unmet needs of biopharma companies as they work to navigate market challenges, optimize capital, demonstrate capability to investors and achieve early and long-term milestones.

Actum is an affiliate of AscellaHealth, a global partner delivering customizable solutions to support the specialty pharmaceutical industry. Executive leaders Donovan and Bob will serve dual appointments, enabling them to provide more comprehensive support to manufacturers that bring crucial treatments to patients with rare and complex medical conditions.

Quill emphasizes, “The stakes are higher than ever for life sciences organizations to ensure they are building a viable and sustainable business to ultimately treat critical patient populations. The unique operating partner approach offered by Actum is a strong complement to the strengths of AscellaHealth, filling critical gaps often experienced by manufacturers as they work to build market penetration and product adoption.”

The launch of this enhanced service portfolio comes at a pivotal time, with historical failure rates of over 90% for clinical drug development, uncertainty in an already complex regulatory environment and a tougher road to public markets.

Gilkin adds, “More than ever, success will depend upon the rapid deployment of cross-functional resources in differing ways, depending on the therapy, disease state and leadership team. Actum’s flexible model offers a modern solution designed to keep pace with today’s evolving healthcare market and it is an exciting time to be bridging the power of both organizations.”

Quill and Gilkin will be joining the existing Actum executive team, including Keri Schoenbrun, chief engagement officer, and Doug Ackley, chief operating officer, who shares, "We are thrilled to welcome Donovan and Bob to Actum. Donovan has a long professional and personal commitment to the rare disease community. Bob has a track record of success in commercial operations and market access. Their complementary skills and experiences will help us to deliver increased impact for our life sciences clients and partners."

About Actum Pharma

Actum Pharma, a strategy and operating business partner to global biopharma manufacturers, is founded and led by industry leaders with a mission to solve an addressable problem in life sciences: the failure of scientifically viable, life-changing therapies to achieve success due to avoidable market missteps. Through a unique operating partner model, Actum helps companies fund, develop, manage and market novel therapies through a portfolio of proven business solutions that accelerate market success. Visit www.actumpharma.com.

About AscellaHealth LLC

AscellaHealth is a global partner that delivers proven end-to-end solutions to both life sciences and healthcare companies to enhance the quality of life for patients with complex, chronic conditions. A dedicated team gets critical healthcare products from manufacturers to patients while ensuring an efficient flow of funds between payers and pharma. Visit www.AscellaHealth.com.

This press release was published by a CLEAR® Verified individual.

data.title: Science in Sport Strikes $105 Million Takeover Deal with Bd-capital Partners
data.description:
(13:41 GMT) *Science in Sport Agrees on Takeover Approach From Bd-Capital Partners
(14:05 GMT) Science in Sport Strikes $105 Million Takeover Deal with Bd-capital Partners

By Mauro Orru

Science in Sport said it reached an agreement on the terms of an offer to be acquired by Bd-capital Partners.

The London-listed sports-nutrition business said Bd-capital tabled an offer of 34 pence a share in cash, valuing it at nearly 79 million pounds ($104.6 million).

"The acquisition will provide SiS shareholders with the opportunity to realize the value of their holdings, in cash, at an attractive value without providing further capital or assuming additional risk likely to be necessary to drive meaningful growth and value creation over the medium term," Science in Sport Nonexecutive Director Henry Turcan said.

The announcement comes a day after Science in Sport said it had received a takeover approach.

Write to Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

Update: Eli Lilly Shares Rise as Potential Diabetes Treatment Meets Primary, Secondary Endpoints
data.description:
(Updates to include Eli Lilly's recent stock move in the headline and first paragraph.)

Eli Lilly shares were up over 15% in recent Thursday trading after the company said its drug orforglipron met primary and secondary endpoints, compared with a placebo, in a phase 3 study evaluating its safety and efficacy in adults with type 2 diabetes and inadequate glycemic control with diet and exercise alone.

Orforglipron met its primary endpoint of superior A1C reduction compared to placebo at 40 weeks, lowering it by an average of 1.3% to 1.6% from a baseline of 8.0%, the company said.

In a secondary endpoint, more than 65% of patients in the study taking the highest dose of orforglipron achieved an A1C less than or equal to 6.5%, below the American Diabetes Association's defined threshold for diabetes, the company said.

In another secondary endpoint, participants in the 6,000-patient study taking orforglipron lost an average of 16.0 pounds at the highest dose, the company said.

data.title: Update: UnitedHealth Shares Fall After 2025 Adjusted Earnings Guidance Cut
data.description:
(Updates with the latest stock movement in the headline and the first paragraph.)

UnitedHealth shares fell 19% in recent Thursday trading after the company reported Q1 results and lowered its 2025 adjusted earnings guidance.

The health care company reported Q1 adjusted earnings of $7.20 per diluted share, up from $6.91 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $7.30.

Revenue for the quarter ended March 31 was $109.58 billion, up from $99.8 billion a year earlier.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $111.54 billion.

For 2025, the company lowered its adjusted EPS outlook to a range of $26 to $26.50, down from its previous range of $29.50 to $30. Analysts polled by FactSet expect $29.72.

data.title: Christian HELP Selects NewOrg's Nonprofit Case Management Software to Streamline Services and Expand Impact
data.description:
Faith-based nonprofit embraces NewOrg's customizable platform to enhance client intake, scheduling, virtual coaching, and real-time reporting.

Washington, D.C.--(Newsfile Corp. - April 17, 2025) - NewOrg Management System, a leading provider of integrated data solutions for nonprofits, announced today that Christian HELP has selected and implemented its case management platform to streamline service delivery, enhance reporting, and support the organization's continued growth across Central Florida.

Christian HELP, a faith-based nonprofit committed to preventing homelessness by connecting individuals with employment resources and essential support, needed a secure and scalable system to replace their previous platform, which had abruptly ceased operations. With over 33 years of service and a track record of assisting more than 170,000 job seekers, the organization required a solution that could be deployed quickly while also adapting to future growth—including plans to replicate services in a neighboring county.

NewOrg delivered a rapid, customized implementation designed to meet Christian HELP's immediate needs and long-term goals, ensuring continuity of services while laying the foundation for expanded impact.

To address Christian HELP's requirements, NewOrg delivered a customized set of features, including:

  • Online Intake Forms - Secure online submissions streamline the client onboarding process while reducing administrative time.
  • Scheduling Tools - Enabling staff to easily coordinate both virtual coaching and in-person appointments.
  • Service Documentation - Centralized tools for tracking services such as food distribution, job search coaching, and referrals.
  • Outcome Reporting - Real-time dashboards and automated reports support grant compliance and strategic planning.
  • Secure Communications - HIPAA-compliant messaging and file-sharing tools enhance client engagement and confidentiality.

"NewOrg's technology has significantly expanded our capacity to serve more individuals. Our coaches can now assist clients with greater efficiency while capturing and analyzing more data than ever before. The ability to track client progress throughout our coaching sessions has not only refined our methodology but also enhanced our overall effectiveness. One of the greatest advantages of NewOrg is its adaptability. As our organization continues to grow and evolve to meet community needs, NewOrg grows with us—seamlessly adapting its software to support new services, challenges, and opportunities."

- Britt Johnson, Assistant Executive Director, Christian Help

NewOrg's implementation included full data migration, configuration of program-specific workflows, and hands-on staff training to ensure a seamless transition.

To learn more about NewOrg's nonprofit management platform, visit www.neworg.com.

data.title: RBC Upgrades Antofagasta Rating Amid Copper Price Volatility
data.description:
RBC Capital Markets upgraded Antofagasta's rating to sector perform, pointing to a "more comfortable" risk profile level amid lower copper price levels.

"Copper will continue to be kicked around by trade war escalations and de-escalations. Our concerns on possible downside scenarios have been tempered by price sensitive demand coming out of China," analysts said in a Thursday note. "However, on the flip side there will be lasting economic damage caused by the uncertainty that likely removes any material upside over the next twelve months."

The research firm also noted that Antofagasta is moving into peak capital expenditures as it continues to invest at its Los Pelambres and Centinela projects, but its balance sheet remains in "reasonable shape" for a potential downturn.

Meanwhile, the company's price target was reduced to 17 pounds sterling from 18 pounds, mainly due to an updated exchange rate forecast for a stronger British pound against the US dollar.

data.title: Trump Meeting With Italy Later Today; Japan, Mexico, China Tariffs Talk Updates; Follow the Latest Here.
data.description:
By Callum Keown

President Donald Trump said the U.S. is "getting rich on tariffs" as he criticized Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell for being "too late" to cut interest rates.

"Powell's termination cannot come fast enough!" he said in a post on social-media platform Truth Social.

U.S. 'Getting Rich on Tariffs', Trump Says

"Oil prices are down, groceries (even eggs) are down, and the USA is getting RICH ON TARIFFS. Too Late [Jerome Powell] should have lowered interest rates, like the ECB, long ago, but he should certainly lower them now," he added.

Trump added that the European Central Bank is expected to cut interest rates today for the seventh time this year.

"And yet, 'Too Late' Jerome Powell of the Fed, who is always TOO LATE AND WRONG, yesterday issued a report which was another, and typical, complete mess!" Trump said.

China Says U.S. Tariffs Make No Sense

The escalating trade war between the U.S. and China was center stage early Thursday. Potential tariff deals with other countries, including Japan, moved forward boosting market sentiment.

China said it will ignore President Donald Trump's tariff numbers game after a White House fact sheet showed Beijing faces levies of up 245% due to its retaliatory actions.

The country's foreign ministry said the 245% rate on certain products "no longer makes economic sense," state-owned news agency Xinhua reported. It follows comments last week by the foreign ministry that the U.S. would become "a joke in the history of [the] world economy" if it keeps imposing higher tariffs. The ministry said the levies were already so high that there's no market acceptance for U.S. goods exported to China.

U.S. and Japan Make 'Big Progress'

Trump said negotiators made "big progress" in talks with a trade delegation from Japan late Wednesday, in signs that deals with other countries are moving forward. "A great honor to have just met with the Japanese delegation on trade. Big progress," Trump said in a post on social-media platform Truth Social.

Japan, one of the U.S.'s biggest trading partners, was due to be hit with 24% reciprocal tariffs earlier this month following Trump's Liberation Day announcement. But last week Trump paused the levies for 90 days to give time for negotiations. Japan is also impacted by a global 25% tariff on autos implemented by the White House earlier this month, because it is home to some of the world's biggest car makers including Honda, Nissan, Toyota.

The country's Nikkei 225 Index jumped 1.4% Thursday.

Temu and Shein to Hike Prices for U.S. Shoppers

Chinese e-commerce retailers Temu and Shein said they will hike prices for U.S. customers from next week due to the escalating tariff war between the world's two largest economies. Both companies said that their operating expenses have increased due to recent changes in global trade rules and tariffs. The changes will come into effect on April 25.

In almost identical statements, the two rivals said they were doing everything they can to keep prices low.

Taiwan Semi Says No Tariff Impact Yet

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing said it hasn't suffered any hit from the tariffs yet, as the chip maker, known as TSMC, reported a 60% jump in first-quarter profit, beating analysts' expectations.

While we have not seen any changes in our customers' behavior so far, uncertainties and risks from the potential impact from tariff policies exist," said Wendell Huang, TSMC's chief financial officer, in a statement.

Write to Callum Keown at callum.keown@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Alcoa falls on  forecasting $90 million tariff hit in Q2
data.description:
** Aluminum producer Alcoa's shares drop almost 3% to $24.35 in early trading after co forecasts $90 million tariff hit in Q2

** Current U.S. duties levied on aluminum imports stand at 25%

** Canadian imports cost almost $20 million in Q1, co says

** Shares rose in post-market trading on Wednesday after Q1 profit beat

** Co said it sources some input materials from Chinese suppliers and expects the current tariffs to hike annual costs by $10 million to $15 million with "no suitable replacement suppliers"

** Up to last close, Alcoa shares recovered 16.4% from an over 4-year low of $21.5 hit last week

** Of last close, stock down 33.6% YTD

Exelon gains on senior leadership appointments
data.description:
** Shares of utility company Exelon rise 1.1% to $46.98

** Carim Khouzami, president and CEO of EXC's subsidiary Baltimore Gas and Electric, is set to assume executive vice president position for transmission and development at Exelon

** Tamla Olivier, senior vice president and chief operating officer of Exelon operating company Pepco Holdings, to be the president and CEO of BGE

** Khouzami and Olivier's new roles will be effective May 1

** EXC says Olivier's successor as CEO at Pepco Holdings will be named in the coming weeks

** Including session's move, stock up 23.5% YTD
